subtopic_detail: Oseltamivir initiation in frail nursing home resident with confirmed influenza A
stem: An 84-year-old woman in residential aged care develops fevers, cough and lethargy 36 hours ago, during a documented influenza outbreak in her facility. Point-of-care PCR is positive for influenza A. She has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and ischaemic heart disease but no liver or significant renal disease (eGFR 58). She has been vaccinated against influenza this season.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78, HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 91% on room air, Temp 38.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Mildly tachypnoeic, scattered wheeze, no focal consolidation; alert and orientated
data_table.Investigations: Influenza A PCR positive; CXR mild bibasal atelectasis, no consolidation; troponin negative; creatinine 96 micromol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate antiviral plan?

options.text: Oral oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days, started today, alongside symptomatic measures and watchful escalation for hypoxia
options.rationale: AU CDNA recommends neuraminidase inhibitor therapy for high-risk individuals such as frail aged-care residents within 48 hours of symptom onset; 75 mg twice daily for 5 days is standard at eGFR above 30. (Source: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units)
source_refs: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units

options.text: Oral oseltamivir 150 mg twice daily for 10 days as an extended high-dose regimen for an outbreak setting in residential aged care
options.rationale: Standard adult dose is 75 mg twice daily for 5 days; routine doubling is not recommended for non-immunocompromised hosts. (Source: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units)
source_refs: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units

options.text: Withhold all antiviral therapy and provide only supportive care, given that symptom onset was more than 24 hours ago in a vaccinated individual
options.rationale: Antivirals retain benefit beyond 48 hours in severe or high-risk influenza; vaccination does not preclude treatment. (Source: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units)
source_refs: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units

options.text: Intravenous peramivir as the first-line antiviral on the basis of mild renal impairment and the convenience of single-dose intravenous therapy
options.rationale: Peramivir is reserved for patients unable to take or tolerate oseltamivir; eGFR 58 does not preclude oral therapy. (Source: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units)
source_refs: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units

options.text: Oral amantadine 100 mg twice daily for 5 days as first-line cover against an A subtype influenza outbreak in this residential setting
options.rationale: Amantadine is no longer recommended due to widespread resistance in circulating influenza A strains. (Source: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units)
source_refs: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units

explanation.summary: Frail older adults in aged care with confirmed influenza receive oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days, started as soon as possible after symptom onset. Treatment remains worthwhile beyond 48 hours in severe or high-risk disease. Renal dose adjustment applies at eGFR below 30. Amantadine is obsolete due to resistance. Outbreak control includes droplet precautions, cohort isolation and post-exposure prophylaxis for residents and staff per CDNA guidance.
key_points: Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days is first-line for high-risk influenza A
Renal dose adjustment of oseltamivir applies at eGFR below 30
Amantadine is no longer used due to resistance
explanation.pearls: Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days started within 48 hours of symptoms reduces complications and length of illness in high-risk adults including frail aged-care residents.

sources.label: CDNA influenza national guidelines for public health units

subtopic_detail: Uncomplicated lower limb cellulitis empirical therapy
stem: A 46-year-old man presents with a 36-hour history of a red, hot, painful lower leg following minor trauma. He is otherwise well, with no diabetes, no immunosuppression and no documented penicillin allergy. There is no fluctuant collection, no necrosis and no crepitus. The leg has well-demarcated erythema below the knee with associated tenderness. He is haemodynamically stable and afebrile.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76, HR 80, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Unilateral right calf erythema with central warmth, no fluctuance or abscess, intact distal pulses and sensation
data_table.Investigations: WCC 11.4 x10^9/L, CRP 38 mg/L; ultrasound shows no abscess or DVT; HbA1c 5.3%

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg six-hourly for 7 days as a first-choice agent because of its broader gram-negative cover compared to flucloxacillin
options.rationale: Cefalexin is an acceptable substitute for non-severe penicillin allergy but is not first-line over di/flucloxacillin in non-allergic patients. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis and erysipelas)

options.text: Oral di/flucloxacillin 500 mg six-hourly for 5 days as first-line outpatient therapy for non-severe lower limb cellulitis in this patient
options.rationale: Streptococci and Staphylococcus aureus drive uncomplicated cellulitis; di/flucloxacillin covers both and is the AU first-line in non-allergic adults with non-severe disease. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis and erysipelas)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading then maintenance per trough level for empirical MRSA cover in a community-acquired skin infection presentation
options.rationale: Empirical MRSA cover is reserved for known colonisation, severe sepsis, recurrent disease or high-risk exposures, none of which apply here. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis and erysipelas)

options.text: Topical mupirocin to the affected area three times daily plus oral analgesia, without any systemic antibiotic therapy initiated at this assessment
options.rationale: Topical therapy alone is inadequate for cellulitis; systemic therapy is required. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis and erysipelas)

options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily as monotherapy given anaerobic activity and a once-daily option, with no addition of antistreptococcal cover at this time
options.rationale: Doxycycline misses many group A streptococcal isolates and is not first-line monotherapy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis and erysipelas)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated cellulitis is typically driven by beta-haemolytic streptococci and Staphylococcus aureus. First-line empirical therapy in non-allergic adults is oral di/flucloxacillin 500 mg six-hourly for 5 days; cefalexin substitutes for non-severe penicillin allergy. IV therapy and broader cover are reserved for sepsis, severe local features or rapid spread. MRSA cover is added only for known colonisation, recurrent disease or high-risk exposures. Limb elevation, marking of erythema and 48-hour clinical review identify treatment failure early.
key_points: Di/flucloxacillin is first-line for non-severe cellulitis in non-allergic adults
Cefalexin substitutes for non-severe penicillin allergy
Vancomycin or clindamycin is added only for severe disease or known MRSA risk
explanation.pearls: 5 days of di/flucloxacillin 500 mg six-hourly resolves most uncomplicated lower-limb cellulitis; mark the leading edge and review at 48 hours for failure to improve.

subtopic_detail: Uncomplicated lower UTI in a non-pregnant adult woman
stem: A 34-year-old non-pregnant woman presents with 48 hours of urinary frequency, dysuria and suprapubic discomfort. She has no fevers, flank pain, vaginal symptoms or systemic features. She has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. There is no recent antibiotic exposure. Bedside urinalysis shows leucocyte esterase and nitrites positive.

data_table.Examination: Suprapubic tenderness only; no flank tenderness, no vaginal discharge
data_table.Investigations: Bedside urinalysis: leucocytes +++, nitrites positive, blood +; midstream urine sent; not pregnant on serum beta-hCG

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 3 days as empirical first-line therapy in this uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection presentation
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolones are reserved for resistant or complicated infections in Australia; not first-line for uncomplicated lower UTI. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: urinary tract infections)

options.text: Single 3 g oral dose of fosfomycin as the empirical first-line agent without any further courses required for treatment of this uncomplicated lower UTI
options.rationale: Fosfomycin is a second-line AU option used where first-line agents are unsuitable; not the routine first choice. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: urinary tract infections)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg once daily for 3 days as empirical first-line therapy in this non-pregnant adult woman with uncomplicated lower UTI
options.rationale: Trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days is the AU first-line empirical therapy for uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant adults; nitrofurantoin for 5 days is an alternative. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: urinary tract infections)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 7 days as empirical first-line therapy for any culture-positive urinary tract infection in adults regardless of severity
options.rationale: IV therapy is reserved for pyelonephritis, sepsis or oral failure; not first-line for uncomplicated cystitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: urinary tract infections)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 14 days as a long course of empirical therapy in this uncomplicated lower UTI presentation
options.rationale: Amoxicillin has high E. coli resistance rates in Australia; trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin are preferred and 14 days is excessive. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: urinary tract infections)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated cystitis in a non-pregnant adult woman is treated empirically with trimethoprim 300 mg orally daily for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg orally six-hourly for 5 days. Cefalexin and amoxicillin-clavulanate are alternatives. Fluoroquinolones are reserved for resistant or complicated infections. Pre-treatment midstream urine for culture is not required for sporadic uncomplicated episodes but is sent in recurrent or complicated cases.
key_points: Trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days is the AU first-line for uncomplicated cystitis
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg six-hourly for 5 days is an equivalent first-line alternative
Fluoroquinolones are reserved for resistant or complicated UTI
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim 300 mg orally daily for 3 days resolves most uncomplicated lower UTI in non-pregnant women; switch to nitrofurantoin in late pregnancy or trimethoprim-resistant isolates.

subtopic_detail: Pharmacotherapy initiation after low-trauma vertebral fracture
stem: A 72-year-old post-menopausal woman presents with sudden mid-thoracic back pain after a minor stumble. Imaging confirms a wedge compression fracture of T7 without neurological deficit. DXA shows lumbar spine T-score -2.8 and femoral neck T-score -2.4. Renal function and dental status are normal. She is not currently on any antiresorptive or anabolic therapy and has no contraindication to oral bisphosphonates. Calcium and vitamin D intake are documented as low.

data_table.Examination: Localised T7 tenderness, no spinal cord features; vitamin D 38 nmol/L
data_table.Investigations: Calcium corrected 2.32 mmol/L, phosphate 1.0 mmol/L, eGFR 76, magnesium 0.84 mmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, PTH 4.2 pmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate pharmacological plan?

options.text: Oral calcium 1200 mg daily and vitamin D 1000 IU daily alone, deferring any antiresorptive agent until a second fracture occurs in the next year
options.rationale: An incident vertebral fracture with T-score under -2.5 mandates antiresorptive therapy; supplements alone are insufficient. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management

options.text: Subcutaneous denosumab 60 mg every 6 months without prior bisphosphonate trial and without any transition plan if it is later discontinued
options.rationale: Denosumab is an option but discontinuation without bisphosphonate cover triggers rebound fractures; first-line is usually an oral bisphosphonate. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management

options.text: Oestrogen replacement therapy as first-line antifracture therapy in a 72-year-old woman more than 20 years post-menopause with established osteoporosis
options.rationale: Menopausal hormone therapy is not first-line for established osteoporosis in older women given cardiovascular and thromboembolic risks. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management

options.text: Oral weekly alendronate 70 mg with daily calcium 1300 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU supplementation, after dental review and adherence counselling for upright dosing
options.rationale: Oral bisphosphonates are AU first-line for post-menopausal osteoporosis with vertebral fracture; calcium and vitamin D repletion plus dental review and upright dosing maximise effectiveness and safety. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management

options.text: Daily subcutaneous teriparatide 20 micrograms as initial anabolic therapy without any prior antiresorptive trial in this established post-menopausal osteoporosis
options.rationale: Teriparatide is reserved for severe disease or bisphosphonate failure under PBS criteria; not first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management

explanation.summary: Post-menopausal osteoporosis with incident vertebral fracture meets criteria for antifracture pharmacotherapy. Oral bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) are AU first-line with weekly dosing on PBS, paired with calcium and vitamin D repletion. Dental review precedes initiation given osteonecrosis risk. Denosumab and teriparatide are subsequent options when bisphosphonates fail or are contraindicated. Hormone therapy is not first-line in older post-menopausal women.
key_points: Vertebral fracture with T-score under -2.5 is an indication for antiresorptive therapy
Oral alendronate or risedronate is AU first-line with calcium and vitamin D repletion
Pre-treatment dental review reduces osteonecrosis risk
explanation.pearls: Alendronate 70 mg weekly reduces vertebral fracture risk by about 45 percent in established osteoporosis; couple with daily calcium 1300 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU when intake is low.

sources.label: RACGP Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management

subtopic_detail: Anticoagulation duration after provoked DVT post-orthopaedic surgery
stem: A 38-year-old man develops unilateral left calf swelling 12 days after open reduction internal fixation of an ankle fracture. He has been weight-bearing as tolerated in a moon-boot. Compression duplex ultrasound confirms a non-occlusive popliteal vein deep vein thrombosis. He has no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, no malignancy, no thrombophilia screen abnormality and no active bleeding. He is otherwise healthy on no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/74, HR 78, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Left calf 3 cm larger than right, mild tenderness, pulses intact, no respiratory features
data_table.Investigations: Hb 142 g/L, platelets 232 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, LFTs normal, INR 1.0; ECG normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate anticoagulation plan?

options.text: Lifelong oral anticoagulation with warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 as definitive therapy for any first proximal deep vein thrombosis episode
options.rationale: Lifelong therapy is reserved for unprovoked or recurrent VTE, malignancy or significant thrombophilia, none of which apply. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand guideline)

options.text: Six weeks of oral apixaban 5 mg twice daily without an initial loading dose and then cessation of all anticoagulation with no further review needed
options.rationale: A 6-week course is insufficient for a proximal DVT; standard duration is at least 3 months. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand guideline)

options.text: Twelve months of oral apixaban then transition to warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 indefinitely for ongoing secondary prevention of recurrent VTE in this patient
options.rationale: Cross-over to warfarin is not standard; provoked first DVT does not warrant indefinite therapy. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand guideline)

options.text: Withhold all anticoagulation in favour of an inferior vena cava filter alone on the basis that this DVT has been provoked by a recent surgical procedure
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is the cornerstone; IVC filters are reserved for active major bleeding or absolute contraindications to anticoagulation. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand guideline)

options.text: Oral apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for a total of 3 months, then stop and counsel about recurrence risk and prevention
options.rationale: Provoked proximal DVT in a non-cancer patient is treated for 3 months with a DOAC at standard dosing; the apixaban induction-maintenance schedule is the AU regimen. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand guideline)

explanation.summary: Provoked DVT in a non-cancer patient is treated for 3 months with a direct oral anticoagulant. Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily is the AU regimen; rivaroxaban with a 21-day loading then 20 mg daily is an alternative. Lifelong therapy is reserved for unprovoked or recurrent disease, malignancy or thrombophilia. Counselling on recurrence symptoms and travel prevention closes the encounter.
key_points: Provoked proximal DVT is treated for 3 months with a DOAC at standard dosing
Lifelong therapy is reserved for unprovoked, recurrent, cancer-associated or thrombophilia VTE
IVC filter is reserved for absolute contraindications to anticoagulation
explanation.pearls: Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for 3 months is the standard AU regimen for provoked proximal DVT in a non-cancer adult.
